annotations.label: Field (informal lead)

anchor.snippet: a field is a set on which addition , subtraction , multiplication , and division are defined

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Field` class formalizes a set with addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division satisfying the field axioms.

annotations.label: Field (informal)

anchor.snippet: Informally, a field is a set with an addition operation

annotations.note: The same `Field` class captures the informal description.

annotations.label: Field (classic axiomatic)

anchor.snippet: Formally, a field is a set F together with two binary operations on F

annotations.note: `class Field (K : Type u) extends CommRing K, DivisionRing K` matches the classic axiomatic definition.

annotations.label: Field as commutative ring

anchor.snippet: Even more succinctly: a field is a commutative ring in which 0 ≠ 1

annotations.note: `IsField` characterizes a (semi)ring as a field via nontriviality and existence of multiplicative inverses, with `IsField.toField` producing the corresponding `Field`.

annotations.label: Field (alternative axiomatizations)

anchor.snippet: Fields can also be defined in different, but equivalent, ways

annotations.note: Equivalent axiomatizations are not all spelled out; `IsField` and `Field` cover the standard variants but other alternative formulations are not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Field of rational numbers

anchor.snippet: Rational numbers have been widely used a long time before the elaboration of the concept of field

annotations.note: Mathlib provides a `Field` instance on `ℚ` via `Rat.instField`.

annotations.label: Field of real numbers

anchor.snippet: The real numbers R , with the usual operations of addition and multiplication, also form a field

annotations.note: Mathlib provides a `Field` instance on `ℝ`.

annotations.label: Field of complex numbers

anchor.snippet: The complex numbers C consist of expressions

annotations.note: Mathlib provides a `Field` instance on `ℂ`.

annotations.label: Field of constructible numbers

anchor.snippet: Real constructible numbers are, by definition, lengths of line segments that can be constructed

annotations.note: No `ConstructibleNumber`/constructible numbers field is defined in Mathlib (the `Constructible` occurrences in Mathlib refer to topology, not straightedge-and-compass).

annotations.label: Field with four elements F4

anchor.snippet: This field is called a finite field or Galois field with four elements

annotations.note: `GaloisField p n` constructs the finite field of order p^n; `GaloisField 2 2` is F₄.

annotations.label: Every field is an integral domain

anchor.snippet: This means that every field is an integral domain

annotations.note: `instance Field.isDomain : IsDomain K` gives the integral domain instance for any field.

annotations.label: Additive group of a field

anchor.section: Additive and multiplicative groups of a field
anchor.snippet: The axioms of a field F imply that it is an abelian group under addition

annotations.note: `Field` extends `CommRing`, which extends `AddCommGroup`, so the additive abelian group structure is automatic.

annotations.label: Nonzero elements form a multiplicative group

anchor.section: Additive and multiplicative groups of a field
anchor.snippet: the nonzero elements of F form an abelian group under multiplication

annotations.note: Via `DivisionSemiring.toGroupWithZero` (and commutativity from `Field`), nonzero elements form a commutative group; `Units K` packages this group.

annotations.label: Finite subgroups of multiplicative group are cyclic

anchor.section: Additive and multiplicative groups of a field
anchor.snippet: Every finite subgroup of the multiplicative group of a field is cyclic

annotations.note: Mathlib's `isCyclic_of_subgroup_isDomain` proves the stronger statement over any integral domain.

annotations.label: Characteristic of a field

anchor.snippet: then F is said to have characteristic 0

annotations.note: `CharP R p` and `ringChar R` formalize the characteristic of any (semi)ring, including fields.

annotations.label: Characteristic is prime

anchor.snippet: the smallest such positive integer can be shown to be a prime number

annotations.note: `CharP.char_is_prime_or_zero` (for integral domains, hence fields) shows that the characteristic is either 0 or prime.

annotations.label: Frobenius is a field homomorphism

anchor.snippet: is compatible with the addition in F (and also with the multiplication), and is therefore a field homomorphism

annotations.note: `frobenius : R →+* R` is defined as a ring homomorphism in characteristic p.

annotations.label: Freshman's dream in characteristic p

anchor.snippet: ( a + b ) p = a p + b p

annotations.note: `add_pow_char` proves `(a + b)^p = a^p + b^p` in a commutative (semi)ring of prime characteristic `p`.

annotations.label: Subfield

anchor.snippet: A subfield E of a field F is a subset of F that is a field with respect to the field operations of F

annotations.note: `structure Subfield (K : Type u) [DivisionRing K] extends Subring K` exactly matches.

annotations.label: Field homomorphism

anchor.snippet: Field homomorphisms are maps φ

annotations.note: Field homomorphisms in Mathlib are just `RingHom` between fields; there is no separate `FieldHom` class.

annotations.label: Field homomorphisms are injective

anchor.snippet: All field homomorphisms are injective

annotations.note: Combined with `DivisionRing.isSimpleRing`, `RingHom.injective` proves any ring homomorphism from a field (a simple ring) to a nontrivial ring is injective.

annotations.label: Prime field

anchor.snippet: A field is called a prime field if it has no proper

annotations.note: No dedicated `PrimeField` predicate appears in Mathlib (only individual prime fields like `ℚ`, `ZMod p`).

annotations.label: Every field contains a prime field

anchor.snippet: Any field F contains a prime field

annotations.note: Mathlib has the underlying canonical maps `Int.castRingHom`/`ZMod.castHom` and characteristic theory, but no packaged statement "every field contains a prime subfield" as a single decl.

annotations.label: Finite field

anchor.snippet: Finite fields (also called Galois fields ) are fields with finitely many elements

annotations.note: There is no dedicated `FiniteField` typeclass; finite fields are encoded as `[Field K] [Fintype K]` and the `FiniteField` namespace collects results.

annotations.label: Order of finite field is prime power

anchor.snippet: Every finite field F has q = p n elements

annotations.note: `FiniteField.card'` states that any finite field has cardinality `p^n` for some prime `p`.

annotations.label: Construction of finite field via splitting field

anchor.snippet: A field with q = p n elements can be constructed as the splitting field of the polynomial

annotations.note: `GaloisField p n` is defined as `SplittingField (X^(p^n) - X : (ZMod p)[X])`.

annotations.label: Finite fields of same order are isomorphic

anchor.snippet: two finite fields with the same order are isomorphic

annotations.note: Any finite field of cardinality `p^n` is `AlgEquiv` to `GaloisField p n` over `ZMod p`, giving uniqueness up to isomorphism.

annotations.label: Commutative ring

anchor.snippet: A commutative ring is a set that is equipped with an addition and multiplication operation

annotations.note: Mathlib's `CommRing` class is the standard formalization.

annotations.label: Characterization of fields among commutative rings

anchor.snippet: fields can be characterized as the commutative rings R in which every nonzero element is a unit

annotations.note: `IsField R` (with `IsField.toField`) records exactly this characterization.

annotations.label: Fields are commutative rings with only ideals (0) and R

anchor.snippet: fields are the commutative rings with precisely two distinct ideals

annotations.note: `Ring.isField_iff_isSimpleOrder_ideal : IsField R ↔ IsSimpleOrder (Ideal R)` is exactly the characterization via having precisely two ideals.

annotations.label: Field of fractions

anchor.snippet: Given an integral domain R , its field of fractions Q ( R ) is built with the fractions of two elements of R

annotations.note: `FractionRing R` and the `IsFractionRing R K` predicate formalize the field of fractions of an integral domain.

annotations.label: Fractions of an integral domain form a field

anchor.snippet: if the ring is an integral domain, the set of the fractions form a field

annotations.note: `IsFractionRing.toField` (and the `Field` instance on `FractionRing R` for an integral domain `R`) formalizes this.

annotations.label: Field of formal Laurent series

anchor.snippet: The field F (( x )) of formal Laurent series

annotations.note: `LaurentSeries R` is defined and shown to be the field of fractions of `PowerSeries R` when `R` is a field.

annotations.label: Residue field

anchor.snippet: If R has only one maximal ideal m , this field is called the residue field of R

annotations.note: `IsLocalRing.ResidueField R` is defined as the quotient `R / maximalIdeal R`.

annotations.label: Maximality of (f) iff f irreducible

anchor.snippet: The ideal generated by a single polynomial f in the polynomial ring R = E [ X ] (over a field E ) is maximal if and only if f is irreducible in E

annotations.note: Stated for principal ideal rings; `E[X]` is a PID so the polynomial-ring case is an immediate specialization.

annotations.label: Subfield generated by an element

anchor.section: Constructing fields within a bigger field
anchor.snippet: there is a smallest subfield of F containing E and x , called the subfield of F generated by x

annotations.note: `IntermediateField.adjoin F S` (with notation `F⟮x⟯`) is the smallest intermediate field containing `S`.

annotations.label: Compositum of subfields

anchor.section: Constructing fields within a bigger field
anchor.snippet: The compositum of two subfields E and E ′ of some field F is the smallest subfield of F containing both E and E ′

annotations.note: The compositum is realized as the `⊔` (sup) of intermediate fields, with `IntermediateField.sup_def` showing it equals `adjoin F (E ∪ E')`.

annotations.label: Field extension

anchor.snippet: by referring to F being a field extension (or just extension) of E

annotations.note: Field extensions are encoded as `[Field E] [Field F] [Algebra E F]`; there is no dedicated `FieldExtension` class.

annotations.label: Degree of a field extension

anchor.snippet: A basic datum of a field extension is its degree

annotations.note: The degree `[F : E]` is `Module.finrank E F`; the infinite-rank case is `Module.rank`.

annotations.label: Multiplicativity of degrees (tower law)

anchor.snippet: It satisfies the formula

annotations.note: `Module.finrank_mul_finrank : finrank F K * finrank K A = finrank F A` is the tower law.

annotations.label: Algebraic element

anchor.snippet: An element x ∈ F is algebraic over E if it is a root of a polynomial with coefficients in E

annotations.note: `IsAlgebraic R x` says `x` is a root of a nonzero polynomial with coefficients in `R`.

annotations.label: Algebraic extension

anchor.snippet: A field extension in which every element of F is algebraic over E is called an algebraic extension

annotations.note: `Algebra.IsAlgebraic R A` asserts every element of `A` is algebraic over `R`.

annotations.label: Finite extensions are algebraic

anchor.snippet: Any finite extension is necessarily algebraic

annotations.note: `Algebra.IsAlgebraic.of_finite` is an instance deriving `IsAlgebraic` from a finite-dimensional extension.

annotations.label: E(x)/E algebraic iff x algebraic

anchor.snippet: The subfield E ( x ) generated by an element x , as above, is an algebraic extension of E if and only if x is an algebraic element

annotations.note: Combined with `Algebra.IsAlgebraic.of_finite`, `IntermediateField.adjoin.finiteDimensional` gives `F⟮x⟯/F` is algebraic iff `x` is algebraic over `F`.

annotations.label: Transcendental element

anchor.snippet: Elements, such as X , which are not algebraic are called transcendental

annotations.note: `Transcendental R x := ¬ IsAlgebraic R x` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transcendence basis

anchor.snippet: A subset S of a field F is a transcendence basis if it is algebraically independent

annotations.note: `IsTranscendenceBasis` is defined as a maximal algebraically independent family.

annotations.label: Existence of transcendence basis

anchor.snippet: Any field extension F / E has a transcendence basis

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the existence of a transcendence basis in `AlgebraicIndependent/TranscendenceBasis`.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed field

anchor.snippet: A field is algebraically closed if it does not have any strictly bigger algebraic extensions

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosed K` asserts every nonconstant polynomial over `K` splits.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of algebra

anchor.snippet: By the fundamental theorem of algebra , C is algebraically closed

annotations.note: `Complex.exists_root` proves every nonconstant complex polynomial has a root, and `Complex.isAlgClosed` packages this as `IsAlgClosed ℂ`.

annotations.label: Algebraic closure

anchor.snippet: A field containing F is called an algebraic closure of F if it is algebraic over F

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosure R K` says `K` is algebraic over `R` and algebraically closed; `AlgebraicClosure k` is a concrete construction.

annotations.label: Artin–Schreier theorem (real closed fields)

anchor.snippet: by the Artin–Schreier theorem , the degree of this extension is necessarily 2

annotations.note: No general Artin–Schreier theorem on the degree of [F̄ : F] = 2 forcing F to be real closed appears in Mathlib (only the special case for ℝ).

annotations.label: Existence and uniqueness of algebraic closure

anchor.snippet: Any field F has an algebraic closure, which is moreover unique up to (non-unique) isomorphism

annotations.note: Existence is `AlgebraicClosure k` (with `Field` and `IsAlgClosure` instances); uniqueness up to isomorphism is `IsAlgClosure.equivOfAlgebraic`.

annotations.label: Field of Puiseux series

anchor.snippet: the algebraic closure of the field F (( t )) of Laurent series is the field of Puiseux series

annotations.note: No `PuiseuxSeries` declaration exists in Mathlib (only `LaurentSeries` is defined).

annotations.label: Ordered field

anchor.snippet: A field F is called an ordered field if any two elements can be compared

annotations.note: Ordered fields are encoded as `[Field K] [LinearOrder K] [IsStrictOrderedRing K]` (replacing the former `LinearOrderedField`).

annotations.label: Artin–Schreier (orderable iff formally real)

anchor.snippet: The Artin–Schreier theorem states that a field can be ordered if and only if it is a formally real field

annotations.note: `IsFormallyReal` is defined and the forward implication `[LinearOrder + IsStrictOrderedRing] → IsFormallyReal` is given, but the converse (orderability of formally real fields) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Archimedean field

anchor.snippet: An Archimedean field is an ordered field such that for each element there exists a finite expression

annotations.note: `Archimedean` typeclass captures the Archimedean property for ordered structures.

annotations.label: Dedekind-complete ordered field

anchor.snippet: An ordered field is Dedekind-complete if all upper bounds

annotations.note: `structure ConditionallyCompleteLinearOrderedField` is exactly a Dedekind-complete linearly ordered field.

annotations.label: Complete implies Archimedean

anchor.snippet: Any complete field is necessarily Archimedean

annotations.note: A scoped instance derives `Archimedean` from `ConditionallyCompleteLinearOrderedField`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of complete ordered field R

anchor.snippet: R is the unique complete ordered field, up to isomorphism

annotations.note: `inducedOrderRingIso` gives a canonical (unique) order-ring isomorphism between any two conditionally complete linearly ordered fields.

annotations.label: Hyperreal numbers

anchor.snippet: The hyperreals R * form an ordered field that is not Archimedean

annotations.note: `Hyperreal` is defined in Mathlib as an ultrapower of ℝ giving a non-Archimedean ordered field.

annotations.label: Topological field

anchor.snippet: Another refinement of the notion of a field is a topological field

annotations.note: Topological fields are formalized via `IsTopologicalDivisionRing` (and `IsTopologicalRing` plus continuous inversion).

annotations.label: Field of p-adic numbers

anchor.snippet: The field Q p is used in number theory and p -adic analysis

annotations.note: `Padic p` (notation `ℚ_[p]`) is defined as the completion of ℚ with respect to the p-adic absolute value.

annotations.label: Local field

anchor.snippet: The following topological fields are called local fields

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes nonarchimedean local fields via `IsNonarchimedeanLocalField` but does not have a single unified `LocalField` class including ℝ and ℂ.

annotations.label: Ax–Kochen theorem

anchor.snippet: An application of this is the Ax–Kochen theorem describing zeros of homogeneous polynomials in Q p

annotations.note: No Ax–Kochen theorem appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Differential field

anchor.snippet: Differential fields are fields equipped with a derivation

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Differential` typeclass (in `Mathlib/RingTheory/Derivation/DifferentialRing.lean`) equips a ring/field with a derivation; the field-specific theory lives in `Mathlib/FieldTheory/Differential/`.

annotations.label: Finite Galois extension

anchor.snippet: finite Galois extensions F / E , which are, by definition, those that are separable and normal

annotations.note: `IsGalois F E` is defined as `Algebra.IsSeparable F E ∧ Normal F E`.

annotations.label: Primitive element theorem

anchor.snippet: The primitive element theorem shows that finite separable extensions are necessarily simple

annotations.note: `Field.exists_primitive_element` proves a finite separable extension has a primitive element.

annotations.label: Galois group

anchor.snippet: the Galois group Gal( F / E ) is the group of field automorphisms of F that are trivial on E

annotations.note: `Gal(F/E)` is realized as `F ≃ₐ[E] F` (the `Gal(F/K)` notation is used throughout `FieldTheory/Galois`).

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory

anchor.snippet: the fundamental theorem of Galois theory , which constructs an explicit one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: `IsGalois.intermediateFieldEquivSubgroup` gives the explicit order isomorphism between intermediate fields and subgroups.

annotations.label: Abel–Ruffini theorem

anchor.snippet: this fact is known as the Abel–Ruffini theorem

annotations.note: `Mathlib/FieldTheory/AbelRuffini.lean` formalizes only one direction (solvable-by-rad implies solvable Galois group); the existence of a non-solvable quintic lives in `Archive/Wiedijk100Theorems/AbelRuffini.lean`.

annotations.label: Galois extension via tensor product

anchor.snippet: a finite extension F / E of degree n is a Galois extension if and only if there is an isomorphism of F -algebras

annotations.note: The characterization of Galois extensions via `F ⊗_E F ≃ F^n` is not present in Mathlib (no matching tensor-product/Galois equivalence theorem found).

annotations.label: Classification of algebraically closed fields

anchor.snippet: Two algebraically closed fields E and F are isomorphic precisely if these two data agree

annotations.note: `IsAlgClosed.equivOfTranscendenceBasis` produces an isomorphism from matching characteristic and transcendence degree.

annotations.label: Elementarily equivalent fields

anchor.snippet: two fields E and F are called elementarily equivalent if every mathematical statement that is true for E is also true for F

annotations.note: Elementary equivalence is defined for general first-order structures and applied to fields in `Mathlib/ModelTheory/Algebra/Field`.

annotations.label: Lefschetz principle

anchor.snippet: The Lefschetz principle states that C is elementarily equivalent to any algebraically closed field F of characteristic zero

annotations.note: The Lefschetz principle is formalized as `FirstOrder.Field.ACF_zero_realize_iff_infinite_ACF_prime_realize`, with completeness of ACF₀ from `FirstOrder.Field.ACF_isComplete`.

annotations.label: Łoś's theorem

anchor.snippet: Łoś's theorem states that any first order statement that holds for all but finitely many

annotations.note: Łoś's theorem is formalized as `FirstOrder.Language.Ultraproduct.sentence_realize` in `Mathlib/ModelTheory/Ultraproducts.lean`.

annotations.label: Absolute Galois group

anchor.snippet: the absolute Galois group Gal( F ) is fundamentally important

annotations.note: `Field.absoluteGaloisGroup K := AlgebraicClosure K ≃ₐ[K] AlgebraicClosure K`.

annotations.label: Absolute Galois group of finite field

anchor.snippet: the group Gal( F q ) can be shown to be the Prüfer group

annotations.note: No identification of `Gal(F_q)` with `Ẑ` (profinite integers / Prüfer group) appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Galois group of finite extension of finite field

anchor.snippet: Gal( F q n / F q ) = Z / n Z

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `Fintype.card Gal(Extension k p n / k) = n` and gives an `IsCyclic` instance generated by the Frobenius, but does not package the explicit `Gal ≃* ZMod n` isomorphism as a single decl.

annotations.label: Brauer group of a field

anchor.snippet: the Brauer group , which is classically defined as the group of central simple F -algebras

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `BrauerGroup` in `Mathlib/Algebra/BrauerGroup/Defs.lean` via equivalence classes of central simple algebras.

annotations.label: Milnor K-theory

anchor.snippet: Milnor K-theory is defined as

annotations.note: Milnor K-theory of a field is not defined in Mathlib (no `MilnorK`/`KMilnor` decl exists).

annotations.label: Norm residue isomorphism theorem

anchor.snippet: The norm residue isomorphism theorem , proved around 2000 by Vladimir Voevodsky

annotations.note: The norm residue/Bloch–Kato theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matsumoto's theorem

anchor.snippet: Matsumoto's theorem shows that K 2 ( F ) agrees with K 2 M ( F )

annotations.note: Matsumoto's theorem on K₂ is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unique solution of ax = b in a field

anchor.snippet: has a unique solution x in a field F

annotations.note: Solvability and uniqueness of `a*x = b` for `a ≠ 0` follows from `GroupWithZero`/field lemmas like `eq_div_iff`, `div_mul_cancel₀`.

annotations.label: Every vector space has a basis

anchor.snippet: the proof that any vector space has a basis

annotations.note: `Module.Basis.ofVectorSpace` constructs a basis for any vector space over a division ring (using choice).

annotations.label: Field of functions

anchor.snippet: form a field, called field of functions

annotations.note: Mathlib has `RatFunc K` (the field of rational functions in one variable); the general geometric notion is covered by `Scheme.functionField`.

annotations.label: Function field of an algebraic variety

anchor.snippet: The function field of an algebraic variety X (a geometric object defined as the common zeros of polynomial equations) consists of ratios of regular functions

annotations.note: `Scheme.functionField` defines the function field of an irreducible scheme; classical varieties are subsumed.

annotations.label: Global field

anchor.snippet: Global fields are in the limelight in algebraic number theory and arithmetic geometry . They are, by definition, number fields

annotations.note: Mathlib has `NumberField K` and `FunctionField F K` separately; there is no single `GlobalField` class encompassing both.

annotations.label: Cyclotomic field

anchor.snippet: Cyclotomic fields are among the most intensely studied number fields

annotations.note: `CyclotomicField n K` is defined as the splitting field of `cyclotomic n K`.

annotations.label: Ostrowski's theorem

anchor.snippet: Ostrowski's theorem asserts that the only completions of Q , a global field, are the local fields Q p and R

annotations.note: `Rat.AbsoluteValue.equiv_real_or_padic` classifies absolute values on ℚ as equivalent to the real or a p-adic one.

annotations.label: Hasse–Minkowski theorem

anchor.snippet: the Hasse–Minkowski theorem reduces the problem of finding rational solutions of quadratic equations

annotations.note: No Hasse–Minkowski theorem appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kronecker–Weber theorem

anchor.snippet: the Kronecker–Weber theorem , describes the maximal abelian Q ab extension of Q

annotations.note: Kronecker–Weber is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Field with one element

anchor.snippet: there is a concept of field with one element

annotations.note: The conjectural F₁ is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Division ring (skew field)

anchor.snippet: Dropping instead commutativity of multiplication leads to the concept of a division ring or skew field

annotations.note: `class DivisionRing` is a ring with multiplicative inverses for nonzero elements.

annotations.label: Quaternions

anchor.snippet: the quaternions H (in which multiplication is non-commutative)

annotations.note: `Quaternion R` is defined in Mathlib; over ℝ it is the standard non-commutative division ring ℍ.

annotations.label: Frobenius theorem on division algebras

anchor.snippet: This result is known as the Frobenius theorem

annotations.note: Frobenius's classification of finite-dimensional ℝ-division algebras as ℝ, ℂ, ℍ is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wedderburn's little theorem

anchor.snippet: Wedderburn's little theorem states that all finite division rings are fields

annotations.note: `Mathlib/RingTheory/LittleWedderburn.lean` proves every finite division ring is a field.

annotations.label: Characteristic p annihilates field elements

anchor.snippet: If F has characteristic p , then p ⋅ a = 0 for all a in F

annotations.note: `CharP.cast_eq_zero` shows that the characteristic, cast into the ring, equals zero; combined with module structure this gives `p • a = 0` for every `a`.

annotations.label: Complex numbers as residue field R[X]/(X²+1)

anchor.snippet: C is obtained from R by adjoining the imaginary unit symbol i , which satisfies f ( i ) = 0 , where f ( X ) = X 2 + 1

annotations.note: No explicit `ℝ[X] / (X² + 1) ≃ ℂ` isomorphism declaration was found in Mathlib (the cited `Complex.isomorphismQuotientXSqAddOne` does not exist).

annotations.label: Transcendence of e

anchor.snippet: Charles Hermite (1873) and Ferdinand von Lindemann (1882) proved the transcendence of e

annotations.note: Only the analytical scaffolding for Lindemann is present (`Mathlib/NumberTheory/Transcendental/Lindemann/AnalyticalPart.lean`); the transcendence of `e` itself is not yet a theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algebraic number field

anchor.snippet: Both Abel and Galois worked with what is today called an algebraic number field

annotations.note: `NumberField K` (finite-dimensional ℚ-extension with `CharZero`) formalizes the notion of an algebraic number field.
